title: Fix headless spawn model selection - pass model to message, not session
description:
Root cause: OpenCode uses per-message model selection (by design), but orch-go passes model to CreateSession (ignored) instead of SendMessageAsync.

**The learning:** OpenCode model selection is per-message, not per-session. This enables mid-conversation model switching, cost optimization, and flexibility. Not a bug - intentional design.

**Fix needed in orch-go:**
1. Update SendMessageAsync to accept model parameter
2. Pass model in payload: `"model": { "providerID": "anthropic", "modelID": "..." }`
3. Remove Model from CreateSessionRequest (it's ignored anyway)
4. Thread model from spawn flags through to first message

**Also consider:**
- Add --model flag to `orch send` for follow-up messages
- Store intended model in workspace/.model for session consistency

**Evidence:** 
- OpenCode run.ts:233-238 passes model to sdk.session.prompt(), not create()
- OpenCode session/index.ts:124-139 create schema has no model field
- Investigation: .kb/investigations/2025-12-23-inv-model-selection-issue-architect-agent.md

**Decision to record:** OpenCode model selection is per-message by design

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text:
## Friction Report: Registry Removal Regression

### What We're Experiencing

1. **orch send doesn't work for tmux agents** - Reports success or improved error, but messages don't arrive
2. **orch review can't find SYNTHESIS.md** - File exists but review reports it missing (orch-go-8b5e)
3. **Sending guidance to agents is broken** - Had to fall back to raw `tmux send-keys` to communicate with debugging agent

### Root Cause Hypothesis

Registry removal (commit b217e39) broke the **beads_id → workspace** mapping that multiple commands depend on.

**Before registry removal:**
- Registry stored: `{beads_id, workspace_name, session_id, window_id}`
- Commands could look up any field to find others

**After registry removal:**
- `findWorkspaceByBeadsID()` was added in Phase 2 but may not work correctly
- `resolveSessionID()` in send command doesn't find sessions for tmux agents
- Review command can't map beads ID to workspace path

### Evidence of Deeper Issue

The Phase 2 commit (c8a83e0) claimed to add `findWorkspaceByBeadsID()` but:
1. Send command still can't find workspaces
2. Review command still can't find SYNTHESIS.md
3. Multiple commands affected = systemic issue, not isolated bug

### What Should Have Happened

Registry removal should have been validated with:
- Test: spawn agent, send message, verify delivery
- Test: spawn agent, complete, verify review finds SYNTHESIS.md  
- Test: spawn agent, run tail/question, verify output

We only tested `orch status` and `orch review` listing - not the full agent interaction cycle.

### Likely Fix

The `findWorkspaceByBeadsID()` function needs to:
1. Scan `.orch/workspace/*/SPAWN_CONTEXT.md` files
2. Parse beads ID from each file
3. Return workspace path when match found

This lookup must be used by: send, review, tail, question, complete, resume

comments.author: dylanconlin

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Complete - Fixed orch send to use findWorkspaceByBeadsID which scans SPAWN_CONTEXT.md for beads ID. Previously it only checked if directory names contained the identifier. Now API path works when .session_id exists. Smoke-tested: orch send orch-go-cjl0 returns 'Message sent via API'.

title: Integrate skillc verify into orch complete
description:
## Problem

`skillc verify` exists but nothing invokes it automatically. Agents can complete without running verification. The gate exists but isn't installed in the doorway.

## Solution

When `orch complete` runs, invoke `skillc verify` against the agent's workspace:

1. Look up which skill was used (from beads issue metadata or SPAWN_CONTEXT.md)
2. Find the skill's .skillc manifest (skill registry at ~/.claude/skills/ or embedded in spawn context)
3. Run `skillc verify <skill-path> --workdir <workspace>`
4. Fail completion if verify fails (exit code 1)

## Implementation

In `pkg/verify/check.go`:

```go
// After existing checks (phase, deliverables, commits)
if skill := getSkillFromSpawnContext(workspacePath); skill != "" {
    if err := runSkillcVerify(skill, workspacePath); err != nil {
        return fmt.Errorf("skill verification failed: %w", err)
    }
}
```

## Considerations

- Need to locate skill manifest from skill name (registry lookup)
- Some skills may not have outputs.required defined (skip gracefully)
- `skillc` binary must be available (check in orch doctor?)
- JSON output mode for parsing results

## Success Criteria

- [ ] `orch complete` runs `skillc verify` when skill has outputs defined
- [ ] Completion blocked if verify fails
- [ ] Clear error message showing what's missing
- [ ] Graceful skip if skill has no outputs.required
- [ ] Works with investigation, feature-impl skills
close_reason: Integrated skill output verification into orch complete. Added pkg/verify/skill_outputs.go with 16 tests. VerifyCompletionFull now checks skill.yaml outputs.required patterns. Graceful skip when skill has no outputs defined.

title: Epic: orch init and Project Standardization
description:
## Goal

Provide a single command (`orch init`) that bootstraps all project components needed for the orch ecosystem, with proper port allocation to prevent conflicts.

## Scope

- Add `orch init` command to orchestrate project setup
- Implement port allocation registry to prevent conflicts
- Enhance tmuxinator config generation with project-specific settings
- Create CLAUDE.md template system for consistent project context

## Out of Scope

- Changes to beads (bd init) or kb-cli (kb init) - we shell out to these
- Migration of existing projects (manual or separate script)
- Web UI for port management

## Success Criteria

- [ ] `orch init` creates .beads/, .kb/, .orch/, CLAUDE.md in one command
- [ ] Port conflicts are prevented via ~/.orch/ports.yaml registry
- [ ] Tmuxinator configs include dev server commands with allocated ports
- [ ] CLAUDE.md templates exist for go-cli, svelte-app, python-cli types

## Investigation

See: .kb/investigations/2025-12-21-inv-scope-orch-init-project-standardization.md
close_reason: Batch close from orch review

title: Add orch init command
description:
## Context

Part of orch-go-lqll: Epic: orch init and Project Standardization

## Task

Implement `orch init` command that bootstraps all project components:

```bash
orch init [--type go-cli|svelte-app|python-cli] [--port NNNN] [--yes]
```

**Behavior:**
1. Runs `bd init` if .beads/ doesn't exist
2. Runs `kb init` if .kb/ doesn't exist
3. Creates `.orch/workspace/` and `.orch/templates/`
4. Generates CLAUDE.md from template (based on --type or auto-detected)
5. Allocates dev port via port registry (Task 2)
6. Creates/updates tmuxinator config with allocated port (Task 3)

**Flags:**
- `--type`: Project type for template selection (auto-detect if not specified)
- `--port`: Explicit port allocation (otherwise auto-allocate)
- `--yes`: Non-interactive mode

**Auto-detection logic:**
- go.mod exists → go-cli
- package.json with svelte → svelte-app
- pyproject.toml exists → python-cli
- Otherwise → minimal

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `orch init` creates .beads/ via bd init
- [ ] `orch init` creates .kb/ via kb init
- [ ] `orch init` creates .orch/workspace/ directory
- [ ] `orch init` generates CLAUDE.md from template
- [ ] Auto-detection works for go, svelte, python projects
- [ ] `--yes` flag skips all prompts
- [ ] Idempotent - safe to run multiple times

title: Implement port allocation registry
description:
## Context

Part of orch-go-lqll: Epic: orch init and Project Standardization

## Task

Implement port allocation registry to prevent conflicts when running multiple dev servers.

**Registry file:** `~/.orch/ports.yaml`

```yaml
allocated:
  orch-go/web: 5174
  orch-go/serve: 3333
  beads-ui-svelte: 5175
reserved:
  - 4096  # OpenCode
  - 3000  # Common dev port
ranges:
  vite: 5173-5199
  api: 3333-3399
```

**New package:** `pkg/port/`

**Functions:**
- `Allocate(project, purpose string) (int, error)` - Get next available port in range
- `Release(project, purpose string) error` - Free a port
- `List() map[string]int` - All allocations
- `IsAllocated(port int) bool` - Check if port in use

**CLI commands:**
- `orch port allocate <project>` - Allocate next available port
- `orch port list` - Show all allocations
- `orch port release <project>` - Free a port

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] pkg/port/ package with Allocate/Release/List functions
- [ ] ~/.orch/ports.yaml created and updated atomically
- [ ] Reserved ports never allocated
- [ ] Vite apps get ports in 5173-5199 range
- [ ] API servers get ports in 3333-3399 range
- [ ] `orch port list` shows formatted table
- [ ] Tests for allocation logic

title: Enhance tmuxinator config generation
description:
## Context

Part of orch-go-lqll: Epic: orch init and Project Standardization

## Task

Enhance tmuxinator config generation to include project-specific server commands and allocated ports.

**Current state:** Auto-generated configs have empty server panes:
```yaml
windows:
  - servers:
      panes:
        - # Dev servers (npm run dev, etc.)
```

**Target state:** Configs populated from project config:
```yaml
windows:
  - servers:
      layout: main-horizontal
      panes:
        - cd web && bun run dev --port 5174
        - orch serve --port 3333
```

**Project config:** `.orch/config.yaml`
```yaml
dev_servers:
  - name: web
    command: "cd web && bun run dev --port {{port.web}}"
    port_key: web
  - name: api
    command: "orch serve --port {{port.api}}"
    port_key: api
```

**Implementation:**
1. Add `.orch/config.yaml` parsing
2. Modify `tmux.EnsureWorkersSession()` to use config
3. Substitute port variables from registry
4. Support layout configuration (main-horizontal, tiled, etc.)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] .orch/config.yaml parsed for dev server definitions
- [ ] Port variables substituted from allocation registry
- [ ] Layout configurable (default: main-horizontal for multi-pane)
- [ ] Backward compatible - empty config works like before
- [ ] Generated tmuxinator configs include actual commands
- [ ] `orch init` creates default .orch/config.yaml

title: Create CLAUDE.md template system
description:
## Context

Part of orch-go-lqll: Epic: orch init and Project Standardization

## Task

Create CLAUDE.md templates for different project types that `orch init` can use.

**Templates by type:**
- `go-cli.md` - Go CLI project (like orch-go, beads, kb-cli)
- `svelte-app.md` - SvelteKit app (like beads-ui-svelte)
- `python-cli.md` - Python CLI project (like orch-cli)
- `minimal.md` - Minimal template for unknown types

**Template location:** `~/.orch/templates/claude/` (user-customizable)

**Fallback:** Embedded templates in orch binary

**Template variables:**
- `{{project_name}}` - Directory name
- `{{project_type}}` - go-cli, svelte-app, etc.
- `{{port.web}}` - Allocated web port
- `{{port.api}}` - Allocated API port

**Example go-cli template:**
```markdown
# {{project_name}}

[Brief project description - fill in]

## Development

\`\`\`bash
make build   # Build binary
make test    # Run tests
make install # Install to ~/bin
\`\`\`

## Architecture

[Describe key directories and packages]

## Commands

[List main CLI commands]

## Gotchas

[Document project-specific gotchas]
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Templates for go-cli, svelte-app, python-cli, minimal
- [ ] Templates embedded in orch binary
- [ ] User templates in ~/.orch/templates/claude/ override embedded
- [ ] Variable substitution works (project_name, ports)
- [ ] `orch init` prompts for description if not --yes
- [ ] Generated CLAUDE.md passes basic validation
close_reason: Created pkg/claudemd with embed.FS templates for 4 project types. Integrated with orch init via --type and --skip-claude flags. All 21 tests passing.

comments.author: dylanconlin

Depends on skillc supporting project-type templates. See skillc repo for related work.

title: Project-declared server ports in .orch/config.yaml
description:
Change port management from auto-allocation to explicit declaration.

Current: ~/.orch/ports.yaml auto-allocates ports globally
Desired: Each project declares ports in .orch/config.yaml

Example .orch/config.yaml:
```yaml
servers:
  web: 5173
  api: 3000
```

Then:
- orch servers list reads from project configs
- orch servers start uses declared ports
- orch init creates default config with available ports
- Global registry (~/.orch/ports.yaml) tracks what's RUNNING, not allocated

Benefits:
- Predictable ports per project
- Config checked into repo
- Same ports across machines
- No auto-allocation magic

title: Swarm Dashboard Bug Fixes (Audit Dec 2025)
description:
## Problem

Playwright tests for swarm dashboard failing with 6 failures out of 19 tests. Manual audit identified several bugs and inconsistencies that need fixing.

## Evidence

Test run: 13 passed, 6 failed
- `filtering.spec.ts`: 2 failures (status options count, duplicate Clear buttons)
- `race-condition.spec.ts`: 4 failures (hardcoded port instead of using playwright baseURL)

Additional issues found during manual code review:
- Agent grid uses index as key instead of agent.id (causes stale data on reorder)
- Svelte 5 runes inconsistently mixed with Svelte 4 syntax across components

## Scope

Fix all 6 test failures and the keying bug. Svelte 5 consistency is lower priority.

## Files Affected

- `web/src/routes/+page.svelte` - keying bug, Clear button duplication
- `web/tests/filtering.spec.ts` - test needs update for 5 status options
- `web/tests/race-condition.spec.ts` - use baseURL instead of hardcoded port
close_reason: Complete - 6→2 test failures fixed, remaining are real bugs tracked separately

title: Fix status filter test: expects 4 options but UI has 5 (includes 'idle')
description:
## Problem

Playwright test `filtering.spec.ts:11` expects status filter dropdown to have 4 options (All, Active, Completed, Abandoned) but the actual UI has 5 options including 'Idle'.

## Evidence

Test failure:
```
Error: expect(locator).toHaveCount(expected) failed
Locator: getByTestId('status-filter').locator('option')
Expected: 4
Received: 5
```

Source code at `web/src/routes/+page.svelte:253-259`:
```svelte
<select id="status-filter" ...>
  <option value="all">All status</option>

```

## Fix

```ts
await expect(options).toHaveCount(5); // All, Active, Idle, Completed, Abandoned
```

title: Fix duplicate 'Clear' button selector ambiguity in tests
description:
## Problem

Playwright test `filtering.spec.ts:85` fails because there are 2 elements matching 'Clear' button when filters are active:
1. The filter bar's Clear button
2. The 'Clear filters' link in the empty state

## Evidence

Test failure:
```
Error: strict mode violation: getByRole('button', { name: 'Clear' }) resolved to 2 elements:
1) <button class="text-xs text-muted-foreground...">Clear</button>
2) <button class="...mt-1 h-auto p-0 text-xs">Clear filters</button>
```

## Fix

Option A: Update test to use more specific selector:
```ts
const clearButton = page.getByTestId('filter-bar').getByRole('button', { name: 'Clear' });
```

Option B: Add data-testid to the Clear button in +page.svelte:
```svelte
<button onclick={clearFilters} class="..." data-testid="clear-filters-button">
```

title: Fix race-condition tests using hardcoded port instead of baseURL
description:
## Problem

All 4 tests in `race-condition.spec.ts` fail because they hardcode `http://localhost:5188` instead of using the playwright config's `baseURL` (`http://localhost:4173`).

## Evidence

Test failures:
```
Error: page.goto: net::ERR_CONNECTION_REFUSED at http://localhost:5188/
```

Playwright config at `web/playwright.config.ts:11`:
```ts
use: {
  baseURL: 'http://localhost:4173',
  ...
}
```

But `race-condition.spec.ts:15,43,58,73` all use:
```ts
await page.goto('http://localhost:5188');
```

## Fix

Replace all hardcoded URLs with relative paths:
```ts
await page.goto('/');  // Uses playwright baseURL
```

This matches how other tests (filtering.spec.ts, dark-mode.spec.ts) work.

title: Fix agent grid using index as key instead of agent.id
description:
## Problem

The agent grid in `+page.svelte` uses array index as the Svelte each block key instead of agent.id. This causes stale data issues when agents are reordered (due to sorting) because Svelte reuses DOM elements incorrectly.

## Evidence

Source code at `web/src/routes/+page.svelte:299`:
```svelte
{#each filteredAgents as agent, idx (idx)}
  <AgentCard {agent} />
{/each}
```

Using `idx` as the key is a known anti-pattern. When sorting changes the order, Svelte may bind old agent data to reused DOM nodes.

## Fix

Use a unique identifier as the key:
```svelte
{#each filteredAgents as agent (agent.session_id || agent.id)}
  <AgentCard {agent} />
{/each}
```

Prefer `session_id` (guaranteed unique from OpenCode) with `id` as fallback for legacy/tmux agents.

title: Standardize Svelte 5 runes usage across dashboard components
description:
## Problem

The dashboard mixes Svelte 5 runes syntax with Svelte 4 reactive syntax inconsistently across components:

- `+layout.svelte`: Uses Svelte 5 (`$derived.by`, `$props`)  
- `theme-toggle.svelte`: Uses Svelte 5 (`$derived`)
- `+page.svelte`: Uses Svelte 4 (`$:` reactive statements, `$store` auto-subscription)
- Stores (`agents.ts`, `agentlog.ts`, `theme.ts`): Use Svelte 4 writable/derived

## Evidence

Prior investigation found that mixing runes with Svelte 4 syntax caused the '0 agents displayed' bug:
- See: `.kb/investigations/2025-12-22-debug-dashboard-shows-0-agents-despite-api-returning-209.md`

The current state is safe (each component is internally consistent), but inconsistent across the codebase.

## Recommendation

Low priority - the current state works. Options:
1. Standardize on Svelte 4 syntax (simpler, matches stores)
2. Migrate to Svelte 5 runes (better reactivity, TypeScript)

Decision should be made intentionally, not ad-hoc.
close_reason: Deferred - low priority, dashboard functional

title: Implement skillc verify command
description:
## Problem

Skillc compiles skills with output constraints (outputs.required patterns) but doesn't verify that agents actually produced the required artifacts. The teeth are missing - skills are reminders, not gates.

## Solution

Add `skillc verify` command that:
1. Loads manifest outputs.required patterns
2. Expands interpolation variables ({date} → 2025-12-25, {workspace} → current dir)
3. Globs for matching files
4. Validates structure (D.E.K.N. block exists, self-review checklist complete)
5. Returns exit code 0 (pass) or 1 (fail)

## Usage

```bash
skillc verify [skill-path]
# ✓ Output exists: .kb/investigations/2025-12-25-inv-*.md
# ✓ D.E.K.N. summary present  
# ✗ Self-review incomplete: 'Did you run a test?' not checked
# Exit code 1
```

## Implementation

Build on existing infrastructure:
- manifest.go already parses outputs.required with Pattern and Description
- checker package already validates checksums, budgets, links
- Add pkg/verifier/verifier.go for output and structure verification
- Add `verify` case to main.go command switch

## Structure Validation (skill-specific)

For investigation skill:
- D.E.K.N. block: `## Summary (D.E.K.N.)` section exists
- Self-review: all `- [x]` items checked (not `- [ ]`)

Structure rules could be defined in manifest:
```yaml
outputs:
  required:
    - pattern: '.kb/investigations/{date}-inv-*.md'
      structure:
        required_sections: ['## Summary (D.E.K.N.)', '## Findings']
        checklist_complete: true
```

## Why This Matters

This makes skillc a **discipline engine**, not just a compiler. The skill becomes self-enforcing - you can't call it done without the gate passing.

Grounded in Gate Over Remind principle: reminders fail under cognitive load, gates don't.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] `skillc verify` command exists
- [ ] Expands {date} pattern to current date
- [ ] Checks file existence via glob
- [ ] Validates D.E.K.N. section exists (for investigation)
- [ ] Validates self-review checklist complete
- [ ] Returns exit code 1 on failure
- [ ] JSON output mode (--json) for pipeline integration
close_reason: Implemented skillc verify command with pattern interpolation ({date}), glob-based file checks, D.E.K.N. detection, self-review checklist validation, and JSON output. All tests pass. Commit: b138bd0

title: Add FAILURE_REPORT.md template and orch abandon --reason flag
description:
From inv-failure-mode-artifacts investigation:

**Problem:** Successful agents create SYNTHESIS.md, but abandoned agents leave only SPAWN_CONTEXT.md. No structured failure artifacts exist. 33 abandoned events in events.jsonl have no diagnostic value.

**Three failure modes identified:**
- Context exhaustion (~60%) - agent makes progress then stops
- External blocker (~25%) - agent asks question, never gets answer  
- Unresponsive/stuck (~15%) - no progress after spawn

**Solution:**
1. FAILURE_REPORT.md template in workspace on abandon
2. `orch abandon <id> --reason 'context exhausted'` flag
3. Auto-comment on beads: `Phase: Abandoned - [reason]`

**Template:**
```markdown
# Failure Report
**Agent:** {workspace-name}
**Issue:** {beads-id}
**Failure Mode:** {context-exhaustion | blocked | unresponsive | other}
**Last Phase:** {phase from beads}

## What Was Tried
## Failure Reason  
## Recommendations for Retry
```

Ref: .kb/investigations/2025-12-21-inv-failure-mode-artifacts.md

title: Enhance kb-reflect skill to propose issues from findings
description:
**Problem:** Current kb-reflect skill is a procedure manual. Agent runs kb reflect, reports findings, but doesn't act. Triage falls to orchestrator manually reading output.

**Proposed Enhancement:**

Agent applies decision trees from skill and produces actionable proposals in SYNTHESIS.md:
- "Archive 3 stale investigations: X, Y, Z"
- "Create decision for topic with 5 investigations" 
- "Fix drift: constraint X outdated"

Orchestrator reviews proposals, approves/adjusts, then issues get created.

**Why this approach:**
- Gate Over Remind: Turns guidance into action
- Surfacing Over Browsing: Agent does triage work, orchestrator just approves
- Matches agent→orchestrator pattern

**Implementation:**
1. Update kb-reflect skill to include 'Proposed Actions' section in SYNTHESIS.md
2. Each proposal includes: finding type, what to do, why, draft issue title/description
3. Orchestrator can approve (agent creates issues) or adjust manually

**Success criteria:**
- Running kb-reflect produces actionable proposals, not just reports
- Orchestrator time reduced from 'read all findings' to 'approve/reject proposals'

**References:**
- Skill: ~/orch-knowledge/skills/src/worker/kb-reflect/SKILL.md
- Discussion: Two-model session on kb ask design surfaced this gap

title: Implement kb supersede command for decision replacement
description:

- Adds 'superseded-by: <new-path>' to old artifact header
- Adds 'supersedes: <old-path>' to new artifact header
- Two-way linking ensures discovery from either direction

title: Phase 3: Evaluate spawn session_id capture
description:
Evaluate if registry storage is still needed for spawn command. Session_id capture is the critical dependency.

Current behavior:
- Spawn creates OpenCode session
- FindRecentSessionWithRetry captures session_id (500ms-2s window)
- session_id stored in registry for later ops

Options:
1. Keep minimal registry (agent_id→session_id only)
2. Store session_id in workspace (SPAWN_CONTEXT.md or separate file)
3. Derive session_id on every lookup (adds latency)

Daemon concurrency limiting also depends on fast active count lookup.

Risk: High - session_id capture timing is critical, concurrent spawn race conditions

Blocked by: Phase 1 and 2 proving derived lookups work

title: orch sessions search - query OpenCode session history
description:
## Problem

Orchestrator sessions (interactive conversations) contain valuable insights but aren't discoverable. Unlike spawned agents (which have workspaces), orchestrator sessions only exist in OpenCode's session storage.

Example: 2025-12-25 session discovered 'teeth check' diagnostic pattern. To find it later, you'd need to remember the date or hope it was captured as a kn entry.

## Evidence

OpenCode persists sessions at:
```
~/.local/share/opencode/storage/message/{session_id}/{message_id}.json
```

Messages contain role, content, timestamps, model, cost, tokens.

## Solution

Add session search capability:

```bash
orch sessions search "teeth check"     # Full-text search message content
orch sessions search --date 2025-12-25   # Filter by date
orch sessions list --recent              # Recent sessions with summaries
orch sessions show <session-id>          # View session messages
```

## Implementation

1. Walk ~/.local/share/opencode/storage/message/
2. Parse message JSON files
3. Search content field (need to check if content is stored - may only be metadata)
4. Return session IDs + matching snippets

## Open Question

Need to verify OpenCode stores message *content*, not just metadata. The sample showed tokens/cost but not the actual text. May need to check OpenCode's storage format more thoroughly.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Can search session history by keyword
- [ ] Can filter by date range
- [ ] Returns session ID + context snippet
- [ ] Works for both agent and interactive sessions

title: Epic: Go orch-cli Phase 1 - Core OpenCode Client
description:
Rewrite orch-cli core in Go as HTTP client for OpenCode API.

## Context
Decision: .kb/decisions/2025-12-18-sdk-based-agent-management.md
POC validated: ~/Documents/personal/orch-go (spawn, Q&A, SSE, event logging work)

## Goals
- Single Go binary for agent lifecycle (spawn, monitor, complete)
- SSE-based completion detection with desktop notifications
- Native Q&A on completed sessions
- Event logging for observability

## Success Criteria
- orch spawn creates OpenCode session, returns session ID
- orch status shows running sessions from SSE
- orch complete verifies + closes beads issue
- Desktop notification on agent completion
- All events logged to ~/.orch/events.jsonl

## Out of Scope (Phase 2+)
- beads-ui integration
- Full command parity with Python orch
- Daemon mode

## Technical Approach
- Wrap opencode run --attach for spawn/Q&A (validated in POC)
- Direct SSE HTTP client for monitoring
- Shell out to bd CLI for beads operations

title: Go client: OpenCode session management
description:
Build Go package for OpenCode API operations.

## Scope
- Session CRUD (create, get, list, delete)
- Message sending (spawn prompt, Q&A)
- Wrap opencode run --attach --format json

## Implementation
From POC (~/Documents/personal/orch-go/main.go):
- BuildSpawnCommand() - opencode run --attach with prompt
- BuildAskCommand() - opencode run --session for Q&A
- ExtractSessionID() - parse sessionID from JSON events

## Deliverables
- pkg/opencode/client.go - OpenCode client
- pkg/opencode/types.go - Event types, session structs
- Unit tests for parsing

## Validation
- go test passes
- Manual: spawn session, verify session ID returned

title: Go client: SSE event monitoring
description:
Build SSE client for real-time OpenCode event monitoring.

## Scope
- Connect to GET /event SSE stream
- Parse event types: session.created, session.status, message.updated, step_finish
- Detect completion: session.status busy→idle transition
- Trigger callbacks on events

## Implementation
From POC:
- SSEClient struct with URL
- ParseSSEEvent() - extract data: lines
- Event channel for async consumption

## Key Events to Handle
- session.status {busy|idle} - completion detection
- session.created - new session tracking
- step_finish - cost/token tracking

## Deliverables
- pkg/opencode/sse.go - SSE client
- pkg/opencode/events.go - event type definitions
- Integration test with mock SSE server

## Validation
- Connect to running OpenCode, receive events
- Detect session completion correctly

title: Complete Phase 4: Remove agent registry file
description:
## Problem

The agent registry (~/.orch/agent-registry.json) was necessary when tmux was the only way to manage agents. Now that OpenCode provides REST API + SSE, the registry causes more problems than it solves:
- Drift between registry, tmux, OpenCode, beads (four-layer reconciliation)
- Ghost agents from stale entries
- 500+ lines of merge conflict, tombstone, locking logic

## Context

From synthesis: .kb/investigations/2025-12-21-synthesis-registry-evolution-and-orch-identity.md

The Dec 6 investigation (orch-cli) proposed phased migration:
- Phase 1: ✅ Store agent metadata in beads comments
- Phase 2: ✅ Beads-first lookup with registry fallback  
- Phase 3: ✅ Registry stripped to minimal tmux mapping
- Phase 4: ❌ Remove registry file entirely

## Solution

1. Update `orch status` to query OpenCode API + tmux directly (no registry read)
2. Update `orch complete` to work without registry lookup
3. Remove registry file creation from spawn flow
4. Delete registry reconciliation code
5. Remove `~/.orch/agent-registry.json` management entirely

## Trade-offs

- `orch status` will be ~100-300ms slower (acceptable for interactive command)
- Fulfills Dec 1 architectural vision: 'orch orchestrates, but doesn't own state'

## Success Criteria

- [ ] `orch status` works without reading registry file
- [ ] `orch complete` works without registry lookup
- [ ] No registry file created on spawn
- [ ] Registry-related code removed from pkg/
- [ ] No ghost agent issues

title: Phase 2: orch-go Python parity
description:
Port remaining Python orch-cli features to Go.

## Missing Features
- [ ] synthesis - Synthesize recent activity

- [ ] stale - Show stale beads issues
- [ ] doc-check/doc-gen - Documentation management
- [ ] lint - CLAUDE.md size checking
- [ ] logs - Command logging viewer
- [ ] history - Agent history with analytics

## After Parity
- [ ] Add deprecation warnings to Python orch-cli
- [ ] Update all skill references to use orch (not orch-py)
- [ ] Document migration guide

## Parent

title: Implement kb ask command for inline mini-investigations
description:
## Problem

Every investigation spawn does semantic query answering (kb context → read → synthesize), but with 30+ second overhead. Dylan's 'how would the system recommend...' pattern revealed the desire for SYNCHRONOUS/INLINE answers without spawn overhead.

## Proposed Solution

Add `kb ask "question"` command that:
1. Runs kb context with question keywords
2. Reads top N matching artifacts (investigations, decisions, kn entries)
3. Sends to LLM with synthesis prompt
4. Returns answer inline (~5-10 seconds)

## Key Trade-off

| Mode | Latency | Artifact | Use When |
|------|---------|----------|----------|
| `kb ask` | ~5s | None (ephemeral) | Quick questions, exploratory |
| `orch spawn investigation` | ~30s+ | Yes (.kb/investigations/) | Questions worth preserving |

## Implementation Notes

- Optional `--save` flag to create investigation artifact
- LLM integration: use same model as opencode default
- Cost consideration: every ask is an LLM call (unlike kb context which is grep)

## Example Usage

```bash
# Quick inline answer
kb ask "how should we sort the swarm map?"

# Save as investigation if answer is valuable
kb ask "how should we sort the swarm map?" --save
```

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Returns useful synthesis in <10 seconds
- [ ] Clear guidance on ask vs spawn decision
- [ ] Optional --save for questions worth preserving

## References

- Investigation: .kb/investigations/2025-12-24-inv-investigate-emerging-pattern-how-would.md
- kn decisions: kn-38b772, kn-3e8bd6

title: Implement orch servers command for centralized server management
description:
Add CLI commands to manage dev servers across all projects from a single place.

**Commands to implement (in order):**
1. `orch servers` / `orch servers list` - Show all projects with port allocations and running status (check lsof for actual binding)
2. `orch servers start <project>` - Start servers via tmuxinator (tmuxinator start workers-{project})
3. `orch servers stop <project>` - Stop servers pane (send Ctrl+C to servers window)
4. `orch servers attach <project>` - Switch to servers pane in tmux
5. `orch servers open <project>` - Open localhost:{web_port} in browser
6. `orch servers status` - Summary: X running, Y stopped, Z total

**Existing infrastructure to use:**
- Port registry: ~/.orch/ports.yaml (pkg/port)
- Tmuxinator configs: ~/.tmuxinator/workers-{project}.yml (pkg/tmux)
- Tmux sessions: workers-{project} with 'servers' window

**Constraints:**
- Must detect actual port binding (lsof -i :{port}) not just allocation
- Don't kill agent windows when stopping servers
- Handle missing tmuxinator configs gracefully

**Reference:** .kb/investigations/2025-12-23-inv-explore-options-centralized-server-management.md

title: Add SESSION_HANDOFF.md template for orchestrator context preservation
description:
From design-minimal-artifact-taxonomy investigation:

**Problem:** Orchestrator sessions end without structured handoff. Next session starts cold, loses context about active agents, pending work, blocking issues.

**Solution:** SESSION_HANDOFF.md template that captures:
- Active agents and their status
- Blocking issues (PRs waiting, bugs, external deps)
- Key decisions made this session
- Recommended next actions

**Template:**
```markdown
# Session Handoff - {date}

## TLDR
[1-2 sentence summary]

## Active Agents
| Agent | Task | Status |
|-------|------|--------|

## Blocking Issues
- [issue]: [why blocked]

## Decisions Made
- [decision]: [rationale]

## Next Session
1. [priority action]
2. [follow-up]
```

**Integration:**
- `orch handoff` command to generate from current state
- Or manual creation in .orch/SESSION_HANDOFF.md

Ref: .kb/investigations/2025-12-21-design-minimal-artifact-taxonomy.md

title: Epic: Pressure Visibility System - Operationalize Pressure Over Compensation
description:
## Goal

Operationalize the Pressure Over Compensation principle by building three layers that make surfacing failures visible and create pressure for system improvement.

## Context

From investigation `.kb/investigations/2025-12-25-inv-pressure-over-compensation-surfacing-mechanisms.md`:
- 8 surfacing mechanisms exist (kb context, hooks, daemon, beads, spawn context, orch status, synthesis, completion)
- 4/8 are passive (require human to invoke)
- Compensation is currently frictionless - human can paste context without friction
- The gap is a 'Pressure Visibility Layer' that detects, surfaces, and learns from gaps

## Scope

**In Scope:**
- Gap Detection: Detect when orchestrator/agent lacks context it should have
- Failure Surfacing: Make gaps visible and painful to ignore
- System Learning Loop: Convert gap observations into mechanism improvements

**Out of Scope:**
- Changing existing surfacing mechanisms (kb context, hooks, etc.)
- Automated context injection (hooks already do this)
- Agent-level introspection (out of scope)

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Orchestrator sees gap warnings when context is sparse
- [ ] Compensation patterns are tracked and visible
- [ ] At least 1 gap converts to system improvement per week (beads issue, kn entry, hook)

## Related

- Principle: `~/.kb/principles.md` - Pressure Over Compensation
- Decision: `~/.kb/decisions/2025-12-25-pressure-over-compensation.md`
- Investigation: `.kb/investigations/2025-12-25-inv-pressure-over-compensation-surfacing-mechanisms.md`

title: Gap Detection Layer - Detect missing context at spawn and completion time
description:
## Context

Part of epic orch-go-re8n (Pressure Visibility System).

The first layer of the Pressure Visibility System: detecting when context is missing.

## Task

Implement gap detection at key moments in the orchestration workflow:

1. **Pre-spawn gap detection:**
   - kb context returns sparse results (<3 matches) → flag
   - Task keywords don't match any knowledge → warning
   - Prior related investigations exist but weren't surfaced → gap detected

2. **Post-completion gap detection:**
   - Agent asked clarifying questions that should have been in spawn context → gap
   - SYNTHESIS.md shows agent 'had to figure out' something documented elsewhere → gap
   - Agent created kn entries that suggest missing context → gap

3. **Session gap detection:**
   - Orchestrator asks for context that kb should have → gap
   - Human pastes context (detected via hook?) → compensation detected

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Sparse kb context warning shows prominently at spawn time
- [ ] Gap detection flags are returned by spawn command
- [ ] At least 3 gap detection heuristics are implemented

## Implementation Notes

See investigation: `.kb/investigations/2025-12-25-inv-pressure-over-compensation-surfacing-mechanisms.md` Finding 5 for three-layer model.

title: Failure Surfacing Layer - Make gaps visible and painful to ignore
description:
## Context

Part of epic orch-go-re8n (Pressure Visibility System).

The second layer: once gaps are detected, surface them prominently.

## Task

Implement visible surfacing for detected gaps:

1. **Spawn-time surfacing:**
   - Show gap warnings prominently (not buried in logs)
   - Consider: block spawn if gap score is too high (gate, not reminder)
   - Show 'you compensated here last session' if pattern detected

2. **Dashboard surfacing:**
   - Add gap visibility to swarm dashboard
   - Show agents that had to compensate
   - Show recurring gap patterns

3. **Session surfacing:**
   - SessionStart hook could show recent gaps
   - 'Pressure report': what the system is missing

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Gap warnings display prominently at spawn time (not just logs)
- [ ] Dashboard shows gap patterns across agents
- [ ] Gaps are harder to ignore than to fix (friction added to compensation path)

## Implementation Notes

Reference Gate Over Remind principle: 'Enforce through gates (cannot proceed without), not reminders (easily ignored).'

Depends on: orch-go-re8n.1 (Gap Detection Layer)

title: System Learning Loop - Convert gap observations into mechanism improvements
description:
## Context

Part of epic orch-go-re8n (Pressure Visibility System).

The third layer: convert observed gaps into system improvements.

## Task

Implement the learning loop that converts gaps into improvements:

1. **Gap → Issue suggestion:**
   - Detected gap suggests beads issue creation
   - 'This gap occurred 3 times - create issue?'
   - Auto-populate issue with gap context

2. **Gap → Knowledge suggestion:**
   - Detected gap suggests kn entry
   - 'Add constraint? Add decision?'
   - Track which gaps become knowledge vs which recur

3. **Pattern analysis:**
   - Which topics frequently lack context?
   - Which skills/spawns have highest gap rates?
   - Recommend hook additions or kb entries

4. **Improvement tracking:**
   - Did the created issue/knowledge reduce gap rate?
   - Feedback loop: improvements that don't reduce gaps → review

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Recurring gaps automatically suggest beads issues
- [ ] Gap → knowledge path is one command
- [ ] System tracks improvement effectiveness

## Implementation Notes

The goal: make the system learn from gaps rather than relying on human compensation.

Depends on: orch-go-re8n.1 (Gap Detection), orch-go-re8n.2 (Failure Surfacing)

title: orch clean --verify-opencode deletes orchestrator's own session
description:
## Problem

`orch clean --verify-opencode` can delete the orchestrator's own active session, causing OpenCode to crash with 'No user message found in stream' error.

The issue occurs in `cleanOrphanedDiskSessions()`:
1. Lists ALL disk sessions for the project directory
2. Gets session IDs tracked via workspace files (`.orch/workspace/*/SESSION_ID`)
3. Deletes any session on disk that isn't in a workspace file

The orchestrator's session is running in the same project directory, is on disk, but ISN'T tracked in a workspace (it's not a spawned agent - it's the main interactive session). So it gets flagged as 'orphaned' and deleted while still running.

After deletion, `SessionPrompt.loop()` continues running but finds no messages → crashes at `packages/opencode/src/session/prompt.ts:273`.

## Evidence

- `cmd/orch/main.go:3064-3069` - Logic that marks sessions as orphaned if not in workspace files
- `cmd/orch/main.go:3093` - `client.DeleteSession(session.ID)` deletes the orphan
- `packages/opencode/src/session/prompt.ts:273` - Crash site: `if (!lastUser) throw new Error('No user message found in stream...')`
- Prior knowledge constraint: 'OpenCode x-opencode-directory header returns ALL disk sessions'

Reproduction:
1. Start OpenCode in orch-go directory
2. Run `orch clean --verify-opencode`
3. Observe: Orchestrator's session is listed as orphaned and deleted
4. Crash: 'No user message found in stream'

## Context

This is a race condition during cleanup. The orchestrator is essentially deleting its own session out from under itself. The fix needs to skip the currently active session when cleaning orphans.

Options for fixing:
1. Skip any session that is currently 'busy' (has active loop running)
2. Pass orchestrator's current session ID to clean and exclude it
3. Check if session was updated within last N seconds (recently active)
4. Query OpenCode for sessions with active status

Recommendation: Option 1 or 4 - use OpenCode's session status to skip busy sessions.

title: Daemon capacity count goes stale - needs reconciliation on each poll
description:
Daemon maintains in-memory count of active agents. When agents complete or are abandoned externally (via bd close, orch clean), the count becomes stale. Daemon thinks it's at capacity (3/3) when actual count is lower.

Had to restart daemon twice this session to unblock.

Fix: Reconcile active count against actual OpenCode sessions on each poll cycle, not just trust in-memory state.
close_reason: Fixed daemon capacity stale count bug. Added Pool.Reconcile() method that syncs internal count with actual OpenCode sessions. Called at start of each poll cycle. 8 new tests added, all passing.

comments.text: Phase: Investigating - Root cause identified: daemon's WorkerPool tracks slots internally but never reconciles with actual OpenCode sessions. Slots are acquired on spawn but never released when agents complete.

title: orch spawn should auto-start OpenCode if not running
description:
## Problem

After computer restart, `orch spawn` fails with 'connection refused' because OpenCode server isn't running. User must manually start OpenCode first.

## Solution

When `orch spawn` detects OpenCode isn't reachable (connection refused on port 4096):

1. Start OpenCode server in background: `opencode serve --port 4096 &`
2. Wait for it to be ready (poll /session endpoint)
3. Proceed with spawn

## Alternative considered

- launchd/plist for auto-start on login - more system-level, less portable
- Require manual start - current behavior, friction after restarts

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] orch spawn auto-starts OpenCode if connection refused
- [ ] Waits for OpenCode to be ready before proceeding
- [ ] Works on both macOS and Linux
- [ ] Logs that it started OpenCode (user knows what happened)
close_reason: Fixed - orch spawn now auto-starts OpenCode if not running, waits up to 10s for it to be ready

title: Fix spawn to use standalone mode with TUI (port Python approach)
description:
## Problem

Workers spawned via orch-go don't show the OpenCode TUI - they run in headless mode with raw text output. This breaks observability and the expected worker experience.

## Root Cause

orch-go uses `opencode run --model X --title Y "prompt"` which passes prompt as CLI argument, triggering headless mode. Python orch-cli uses standalone mode which renders full TUI.

## Current (broken)
```
opencode run --model anthropic/claude-opus --title og-feat-xyz "prompt"
# Runs headless, no TUI
```

## Expected (Python's approach)
```
opencode {project_dir} --model anthropic/claude-opus
# Wait for TUI ready (poll for visual indicators)
# Wait 1.0s for input focus
tmux send-keys -l "prompt"
tmux send-keys Enter
# Full TUI rendered
```

## Implementation

Port from Python orch-cli (spawn.py:837-1127):

1. **WaitForOpenCodeReady()** - Poll tmux pane for TUI indicators:
   - `┃` (thick vertical bar - prompt box)
   - `build` or `agent` (agent selector)
   - `alt+x` or `commands` (command hints)
   - Timeout: 15s, Poll interval: 200ms
   - Extra 1.0s wait after visual ready for input focus

2. **Modify BuildRunCommand()** - Change from:
   ```go
   args := []string{"run", "--model", cfg.Model, "--title", cfg.Title, cfg.Prompt}
   ```
   To standalone mode:
   ```go
   args := []string{cfg.ProjectDir, "--model", cfg.Model}
   ```

3. **Add send-keys prompt injection** - After TUI ready:
   ```go
   tmux.SendKeysLiteral(window, prompt)  // -l flag for special chars
   tmux.SendEnter(window)
   ```

4. **Optional: DiscoverSession()** - Query API to find session by directory + spawn time for later API interactions

## Reference

- Investigation: .kb/investigations/2025-12-20-design-explore-tradeoffs-orch-opencode-integration.md
- Python impl: orch-cli/src/orch/spawn.py:959-1032 (spawn_with_opencode)
- Python impl: orch-cli/src/orch/spawn.py:1083-1127 (_wait_for_opencode_ready)

## Validation

- Spawn agent via `orch spawn investigation "test"`
- Verify TUI renders in tmux window (header, token counter, purple status bar)
- Verify agent processes prompt correctly

title: Epic: Orch Ecosystem Consolidation (6-month)
description:
Consolidate 8 repos to 4 functional units. See .kb/investigations/2025-12-24-inv-full-ecosystem-audit-scope-simplify.md for full analysis.

## Phases

### Phase 1 (Month 1): kb absorbs kn
- Add kb quick subcommand with kn semantics
- Migrate .kn entries to .kb format  
- Deprecate kn binary

### Phase 2 (Month 2-3): orch-go reaches Python parity
- Port remaining Python features (synthesis, transcript, friction, stale, doc-check, lint, logs, history)
- Add deprecation warnings to Python orch-cli
- Update skill references

### Phase 3 (Month 4): beads abstraction
- Create pkg/beads/client.go interface
- Reduce direct bd CLI calls
- Enable mock testing

### Phase 4 (Month 5-6): Final cleanup
- Archive Python orch-cli
- Evaluate agentlog (archive or integrate)
- Consider skillc integration into orch-go

## Success Criteria
- Single knowledge CLI (kb) instead of two
- orch-go as only orchestration CLI
- All skill references point to valid tools
- beads interaction via abstraction layer

## Evidence
- orch-go: 37k LoC, 334 skill refs
- kb+kn: 231 combined skill refs, conceptual overlap
- agentlog: only 14 skill refs (orphaned)
- Python orch-cli: ~80% parity achieved

title: Orch Ecosystem Identity & Consolidation
description:
Strategic questions surfaced from ecosystem audit that need decisions before implementation work proceeds.

## Context
Four investigations completed 2025-12-24:
- inv-what-orch-ecosystem-reflect-what.md (identity/philosophy)
- inv-full-ecosystem-audit-scope-simplify.md (8-repo consolidation)
- inv-follow-up-ecosystem-audit-orch.md (beads dependency)
- inv-addendum-ecosystem-audit-opencode.md (OpenCode as runtime infra)

## Open Questions

### Strategic (require Dylan input)
1. **Sharing Philosophy** - Patterns only? Tools as OSS? Personal only? Selective extraction?
2. **Identity Framing** - Is 'amnesia-resilient AI orchestration framework' the right framing?
3. **Stabilization vs Evolution** - Lock patterns or keep discovering?

### Structural (have recommendations, need confirmation)
4. **kb + kn merger** - 231 combined skill refs, significant overlap. Merge?
5. **agentlog fate** - 14 skill refs, low adoption. Archive or integrate?
6. **beads abstraction** - Proceed with pkg/beads/client.go interface?

## Evidence Base
- 1,195 beads issues, 1,179 workspaces, 265+ investigations
- 6 principles documented in ~/.kb/principles.md
- 8 repos audited with LoC, ownership, usage metrics

## Deliverable
Decision record capturing answers to all 6 questions, enabling consolidation work to proceed.
close_reason: All 6 strategic decisions resolved. Sharing: selective extraction (principles as writing). Stabilization: core stable, edges evolve. kb+kn: merge under kb. agentlog: archive. beads: proceed with abstraction. source-of-truth: hybrid (beads for tracked work, OpenCode for running sessions). Implementation work can now proceed.

Spawned provenance investigation: orch-go-upy4

Tracing each principle to its originating failure. This may inform:

title: Decide: Sharing Philosophy
description:
**Question:** How should the orch ecosystem be shared (if at all)?

## Options
A) **Patterns, not tools** - Share principles/docs (like 12-factor manifesto), keep tools personal
B) **Tools as OSS** - Open source orch-go, kb-cli, etc. (significant maintenance burden)
C) **Personal only** - Keep everything private, focus on personal productivity
D) **Selective extraction** - Extract reusable patterns into docs (public), keep tools private

## Context
- Current state: Tools are private, orch-knowledge README describes selective sharing
- 390+ artifacts reflecting Dylan's decisions/workflows/preferences
- Principles document is already publishable quality
- Tools are deeply personal (not designed for others)

## Considerations
- Maintenance burden of OSS
- Value of sharing vs cost of support
- What form would sharing take? (blog posts? manifesto? repo?)

## Deliverable
Decision record with chosen option and rationale.
close_reason: Decision: Selective extraction - share principles/patterns as writing (blog posts, manifesto-style docs), keep tools private. Grounded in Session Amnesia (tools optimized for Dylan's workflow) and Reflection Before Action (avoid support burden). Concrete form: blog post series on principles, not a repo.

title: Decide: Identity Framing
description:
**Question:** What is the right identity/framing for the orch ecosystem?

## Options
- 'Amnesia-resilient AI orchestration framework'
- 'AI agent session infrastructure'
- 'LLM-first development environment'
- 'Personal AI orchestration system'
- Other?

## Investigation Finding
This is **infrastructure for AI agent sessions** - equivalent of:
- dotfiles → configure shells → this configures AI sessions
- CI/CD → orchestrates builds → this orchestrates agent work
- observability → tracks services → this tracks agent sessions

## Key Insight
The core constraint is Session Amnesia - every pattern exists to compensate for Claude having no memory between sessions. This isn't a productivity tool or project management system.

## Deliverable
Chosen identity framing for use in docs, README, and communication.
close_reason:
Decided: 'Amnesia-resilient AI orchestration framework'

## Rationale
- Positions the core constraint (session amnesia) as the defining feature
- 'Framework' signals something with structure/patterns others could learn from
- Technical framing that speaks to engineers who understand the problem
- Accurate to what the system actually is

## Implications for other decisions
- Sharing: Favors sharing patterns/philosophy (the 'amnesia-resilient' insight is transferable)
- Stabilization: Framework implies some stability expectation - core patterns should be documented
- Identity should appear in: README, ECOSYSTEM.md, principles preamble

title: Decide: Stabilization vs Evolution
description:
**Question:** Should the system stabilize or keep evolving?

## Options
- **Stabilize** - Lock patterns, document thoroughly, make accessible
- **Keep evolving** - Continue discovering through practice
- **Both** - Stabilize core, evolve edges (define what's core vs edge)

## Context
- orch-go rewrite just completed (active evolution)
- 6 principles now documented (some stabilization achieved)
- Skills actively being refined
- Consolidation work pending (kb+kn, agentlog fate)

## Considerations
- What's 'core' that should be stable? (principles? skill structure? CLI interface?)
- What's 'edge' that can keep evolving? (individual skills? workflow patterns?)
- Does stabilization enable or hinder sharing?

## Deliverable
Decision on stabilization scope and timeline.
close_reason: Decision: Both - stabilize core, evolve edges. Core (stable): 6 principles, five-concern architecture, SPAWN_CONTEXT structure, D.E.K.N. format. Edges (evolve): individual skills, CLI flags/UX, dashboard, integration patterns. Grounded in Evolve by Distinction - can't stabilize what you haven't discovered.

title: Decide: kb + kn Merger
description:
**Question:** Should kb and kn merge into a single knowledge CLI?

## Evidence
- kb: 123 skill references, long-form artifacts (investigations, decisions)
- kn: 108 skill references, quick entries (decide, tried, constrain)
- Combined: 231 references, significant conceptual overlap
- kn entries promote to kb artifacts (explicit promotion path)

## Recommendation from Audit
Merge under kb with subcommands:
- `kb quick decide` (was `kn decide`)
- `kb quick tried` (was `kn tried`)
- `kb quick constrain` (was `kn constrain`)

Or absorb into orch-go as `orch kb` / `orch kn`.

## Trade-offs
- **Merge:** Simpler mental model, one binary, unified search
- **Keep separate:** kn is faster for quick capture, different UX

## Deliverable
Decision on merger approach and timeline.
close_reason: Decision: Merge kn into kb. Structure: kb decide/tried/constrain for quick capture, kb create for long-form. Key constraint: quick commands stay quick (no prompts). Grounded in Surfacing Over Browsing - one tool to surface all knowledge. Removes unnecessary seam while preserving entry velocity.

title: Decide: agentlog Fate
description:
**Question:** Should agentlog be archived, integrated, or revived?

## Evidence
- Only 14 skill references (vs 334 for orch, 307 for bd)
- Last activity: Dec 16 (8 days ago)
- No integration hooks with orch spawn
- Original purpose: 'AI-first CLI for error visibility'
- No evidence of active use in spawn contexts

## Options
A) **Archive** - It was a good idea that didn't get adopted. Move on.
B) **Integrate** - Fold into orch-go as `orch log` subcommand
C) **Revive** - Add spawn hooks, make it automatic, increase adoption

## Considerations
- Is agent error logging still valuable? (yes, probably)
- Why didn't it get adopted? (no automatic integration)
- Is the effort to revive worth it vs other priorities?

## Deliverable
Decision on agentlog's fate with rationale.
close_reason: Decision: Archive agentlog. 14 skill refs vs 334 for orch = empirical non-adoption. Was a reminder, not a gate. Grounded in Gate Over Remind - if error visibility matters, fold into existing gates (spawn context, complete verification), don't revive a failed separate tool.

title: Decide: Beads Abstraction Layer
description:
**Question:** Proceed with pkg/beads/client.go interface abstraction?

## Recommendation from Audit
Yes - create abstraction layer wrapping the 7 bd commands orch-go uses:
- comment, comments, create, list, ready, show, stats

## Evidence
- beads is external dependency (stevey's project, not Dylan's)
- 31MB binary, 347MB repo, complex internal architecture
- 19 exec.Command calls in orch-go using only 7 of 30+ commands
- Interface is already narrow - abstraction is low-cost

## Benefits
- Isolates API changes to single package
- Enables mock testing for daemon/verify
- Reduces coupling to external project

## Trade-offs Accepted
- Still dependent on external project
- No control over beads roadmap

## Deliverable
Confirmation to proceed (or alternative approach).
close_reason: Decision: Proceed with pkg/beads/client.go abstraction layer. Grounded in Graceful Degradation (enables RPC→CLI→sqlite fallback) and Compose Over Monolith (beads is external, abstraction is the swap seam). Already in progress via orch-go-7yrh RPC client work.

title: Policy skills loading for workers despite audience:orchestrator
description:
Workers spawned with feature-impl are getting the orchestrator skill (1224 lines) loaded, causing:
1. Token limit errors (206587 > 200000 max)
2. Wasted context window
3. Confusing guidance for workers

The orchestrator skill has:
- skill-type: policy
- audience: orchestrator  
- spawnable: false

But OpenCode's skill system doesn't respect these fields - it loads all policy skills regardless.

Options:
1. Fix OpenCode to filter by audience field
2. Move orchestrator skill out of policy/ to prevent auto-loading
3. Trim orchestrator skill size (currently 1224 lines)

Discovered when og-feat-real-time-agent-22dec hit token limit on first response.

title: Add --max-agents concurrency limit to orch spawn
description:
From inv-add-concurrency-limit investigation:

**Problem:** Without limits, can end up with 75+ agents (happened this session). Causes resource exhaustion and makes status unusable.

**Solution:**
1. Add `--max-agents` flag (default 5)
2. Add `ORCH_MAX_AGENTS` env var override
3. Pre-spawn check: if active >= max, block with message
4. `--force` flag to bypass limit

**Implementation:**
- Registry has ListActive() already
- Add count check before spawn
- Clear error message: 'Max agents (5) reached. Use --force to override or complete existing agents.'

Ref: .kb/investigations/2025-12-21-inv-add-concurrency-limit-orch-spawn.md

title: System Self-Reflection - Temporal Pattern Awareness
description:
The system addresses session amnesia but lacks project-lifetime memory. Artifacts accumulate without awareness of their relationships, evolution, or potential for synthesis. Humans must notice patterns manually.

Goal: Enable the system to surface temporal patterns, citation networks, and synthesis candidates autonomously.

Key questions:
1. How do artifacts become aware of each other across time?
2. What signals should trigger autonomous reflection?
3. Can the system ask questions only its artifacts can show?

Success criteria:
- System can surface 'you have 5 investigations about X with no synthesis' without human noticing first
- Citation/reference counting enables emergent hierarchy (load-bearing artifacts visible)
- Temporal drift detection identifies when decisions no longer match practice
- Chronicle artifact captures decision evolution narratives

Builds on: orch-go-4kwt (Amnesia-Resilient Artifact Architecture) - that epic answered 'what artifacts exist', this asks 'how do artifacts become aware of each other across time'

title: When and how to question inherited constraints
description:
The system inherits constraints (local-first, compose-over-monolith) from principles. When is it healthy to question them?

Questions:
- What signals indicate a constraint may no longer serve?
- How to distinguish 'constraint is wrong' from 'we're misapplying it'?
- Should constraints have expiration dates or review triggers?
- How to apply 'evolve by distinction' to constraints themselves?

Example: Local-first constraint
- Made sense for single-user, single-machine
- May break at scale (distributed state, real-time collaboration)
- Question isn't 'files vs database' but 'what are we conflating?'

Possible distinctions hiding in local-first:
- Hot state vs cold state
- Single-writer vs multi-writer
- Operational vs knowledge

title: Design: kb reflect command specification
description:
Design a command that surfaces synthesis/deprecation candidates.

Proposed interface:
  kb reflect                    # Show all reflection candidates
  kb reflect --type synthesis   # Investigations needing synthesis
  kb reflect --type stale       # Decisions with no recent citations
  kb reflect --type drift       # Practice diverged from decision
  kb reflect --type promote     # kn entries ready for kb promotion

Output should:
- Explain WHY it's surfacing each candidate
- Provide actionable next step (spawn synthesis? deprecate? review?)
- Support --json for automation

Dependencies:
- Citation mechanisms (orch-go-ws4z.1)
- Temporal signals (orch-go-ws4z.2)

title: Temporal signals for autonomous reflection
description:
What signals should trigger the system to suggest reflection/synthesis?

Candidate signals:
- Temporal density: N investigations in M days about related topics
- Repeated constraints: same kn constraint across multiple projects
- Citation convergence: multiple investigations reference same finding
- Contradictions: Investigation A says X, Investigation B says not-X
- Staleness: decision with 0 citations, superseded by later work
- Concept drift: decision from N months ago, practice has diverged

Questions:
- Which signals have highest value/noise ratio?
- How to detect 'related topics' across artifacts?
- What's the trigger mechanism? (hook? daemon? command?)

- What's the structure? Timeline? Narrative? Graph?

Example use case:

- Should trace: failures → kn constraints → recurring pattern → principle

title: Add pre-spawn kb context check to orch spawn
description:
Root cause fix for investigation agents ignoring existing decisions.

**Problem:** Orchestrator spawned investigations without checking kb context first. Agents concluded 'no migration needed' without knowing skillc's stated vision (in decisions) was to compile all artifacts including SKILL.md.

**Solution:** Add pre-spawn hook to orch spawn that:
1. Extracts keywords from task description
2. Runs kb context '<keywords>' 
3. If matches found → display them, require acknowledgment (y/N)
4. Include matched context in SPAWN_CONTEXT.md automatically

**Why hook, not discipline:** The orchestrator skill already says to check kb context (line 820). It wasn't followed. Hooks enforce, discipline hopes.

**Implementation:**
- Add to cmd/orch/main.go spawn command flow
- Call kb context with task keywords
- Parse output for decisions/investigations
- Show summary, prompt for acknowledgment
- Add --skip-context-check flag for explicit bypass

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] orch spawn shows relevant decisions before spawning
- [ ] Requires y/N if matches found
- [ ] Matched context auto-included in spawn prompt
- [ ] --skip-context-check bypasses (documented in git)

title: Set ORCH_WORKER=1 in headless spawn to skip orchestrator skill loading
description:
## Problem

Worker spawns unnecessarily load orchestrator skill (1,251 lines, ~37k tokens) via session-context plugin. This wastes context budget and contributed to 207k token explosion.

## Solution

Set ORCH_WORKER=1 environment variable in runSpawnHeadless (cmd/orch/main.go:1147) before spawning.

## Evidence

Investigation orch-go-4gb9 found session-context.js:59 auto-loads orchestrator skill for all orch projects. Workers don't need orchestrator guidance - they have their own skill embedded in SPAWN_CONTEXT.md.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] runSpawnHeadless sets ORCH_WORKER=1 before spawn
- [ ] Verify orchestrator skill not loaded in worker sessions
- [ ] Document in CLAUDE.md

title: Web UI shows 0 agents despite active OpenCode sessions
description: The swarm dashboard at localhost:5188 shows '0 agents' even when sessions are actively running. Root cause: OpenCode sessions have status=null (not 'running' or 'pending'), and the /api/agents endpoint filtering logic doesn't account for null status. Evidence: curl shows 413 non-completed sessions in OpenCode, but /api/agents returns empty array. Sessions with null status are actually active (have recent messages, reasoning in progress). Fix needed: Update agent filtering in serve command to treat null status as active.
close_reason: Batch close from orch review

comments.text: Phase: Investigation - Root cause found: serve process runs with CWD=/ so os.Getwd() returns '/' instead of project dir. ListSessions filters to directory='/' which returns only 12 sessions instead of 415. Fix: Don't filter by directory in /api/agents endpoint.

comments.author: dylanconlin
comments.text: Phase: Implementing - Fixed serve.go to not filter by directory (pass empty string to ListSessions). Also fixed nil slice issue to return [] instead of null.

title: Add Beads stats to dashboard stats bar
description:
Phase 1a: Add ready/blocked/in-progress counts from beads to stats bar.

## Implementation

1. New API endpoint: GET /api/beads
   - Shell out to `bd stats --json`
   - Return BeadsAPIResponse{Ready, InProgress, Blocked}
   - Cache with 30s TTL to avoid CLI overhead

2. Stats bar display:

   - Tooltip with full breakdown

## Files
- cmd/orch/serve.go - Add handleBeads endpoint
- web/src/routes/+page.svelte - Add beads display to stats bar

## Success Criteria
- Orchestrator sees ready work count at a glance
- No significant performance impact (<2s polling)

title: Completed agent cards should show full TLDR/summary
description:
## Problem

Completed agent cards show truncated summaries. The current display cuts off useful information with '...'

## Evidence

Screenshot shows completed agents with summaries like:
- 'Analyzed swarm dashboard session display strategy. Recommended progressive disclosure with Active/...'
- 'Fixed headless spawn to use CLI subprocess instead of HTTP API, enabling proper model selection. The...'

## Question

Can/should we display the whole TLDR? Options:
1. Expand card height for completed agents to fit full summary
2. Show full summary on hover/click
3. Multi-line truncation with 'show more' expansion
4. Slide-out panel on click (already planned?)

## Recommendation

Completed agents are reference material - users want to see what was accomplished. Consider:
- Taller cards for completed section
- Or expandable summary area

## Files
- web/src/routes/+page.svelte - AgentCard component for completed state
